title: Listeria monocytogenes: foodborne meningitis
vignette: A 58-year-old man comes to the emergency department with fever, headache, and nausea for the past day. He underwent kidney transplantation last year. His temperature is 39.4 C (103 F), blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg, pulse is 98/min, and respirations are 16/min. Neck stiffness is present on physical examination. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) results show glucose 40 mg/dL (normal 40-70), protein 80 mg/dL (normal 15-45), leukocytes 900/mm3 (normal 0-5), and neutrophils 80%. Gram-positive rods with tumbling motility at room temperature are seen on CSF microscopy. This patient's infection was most likely transmitted through which of the following mechanisms?

clues.note: A transplant means chronic immunosuppression (impaired cell-mediated immunity). Listeria is an intracellular pathogen controlled by T cells/macrophages, so transplant recipients, neonates, pregnant women, and the elderly are the classic at-risk hosts.
Fever + headache + nuchal rigidity = bacterial meningitis. Combined with the CSF picture it sets up the 'which organism / how transmitted' question.
Neutrophil-predominant pleocytosis with high protein (80 / normal 15-45) and low-normal glucose (40 / normal 40-70) is bacterial meningitis. This frames the search around a bacterial cause.

clues.text: Gram-positive rods with tumbling motility at room temperature
clues.note: Gram-positive ROD + tumbling motility (actin-based, best at ~25 C) = Listeria monocytogenes. It's a facultative intracellular bacterium that also grows in the cold (refrigeration). This is the species-defining clue.

whatMatters: The immunosuppression + bacterial-meningitis CSF set up the organism, and the gram-positive rod with tumbling motility nails Listeria monocytogenes. The actual question is the route of transmission: Listeria is FOODBORNE (unpasteurized dairy, deli meats, soft cheeses) — answer B.
strategy: Two-step microbiology stems: first ID the bug from the lab fingerprint (GPR + tumbling motility + cold growth = Listeria), then answer the specific sub-question (here, transmission). Don't stop at the diagnosis — reread the last sentence to see what's actually being asked.

eliminations.label: arthropod bite — wrong vector class
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: arthropod-borne infections include malaria (Anopheles), Lyme (Ixodes), Rickettsia (ticks), Chagas (reduviid). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: none are gram-positive tumbling rods, and Listeria is not vector-borne; it's ingested. ✅ Correct if: the organism were a Plasmodium, Borrelia, or Rickettsia with a bite-exposure history.

eliminations.label: needlestick — bloodborne viruses, not Listeria
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: needlesticks transmit HIV, hepatitis B, and hepatitis C. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Listeria isn't a classic percutaneous/bloodborne pathogen; the clue is a gram-positive rod from food. ✅ Correct if: the question targeted occupational exposure to HBV/HCV/HIV.

eliminations.label: respiratory droplets — wrong portal
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: droplet meningitis pathogens include Neisseria meningitidis (GN diplococcus), Streptococcus pneumoniae (GP diplococcus), and Haemophilus influenzae. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: those match different Gram morphologies; Listeria is a rod acquired by ingestion, not inhalation. ✅ Correct if: the smear showed gram-negative diplococci (Neisseria) or gram-positive diplococci (pneumococcus).

eliminations.label: unprotected sex — wrong route
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: sexually transmitted organisms include Treponema, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia, HIV, HPV. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: Listeria is foodborne (and can cross the placenta), not sexually transmitted. ✅ Correct if: the stem described a genital lesion/discharge or an STI-associated organism.

reasoning: Immunosuppressed transplant patient with bacterial meningitis
Gram-positive rod + tumbling motility + cold growth = Listeria monocytogenes
Listeria is acquired by ingesting contaminated food
Transmission = contaminated food (unpasteurized dairy/deli meats) → answer B
mechanism: Listeria monocytogenes is a facultative anaerobic, motile (actin rocket / 'tumbling') gram-positive rod that grows at refrigeration temperatures. It is acquired by eating contaminated foods (unpasteurized milk/soft cheeses, deli meats, raw vegetables) and is a facultative intracellular pathogen cleared by cell-mediated immunity — hence disease in neonates, pregnant women, the elderly, and immunosuppressed transplant patients. It can also cross the placenta (transplacental) causing neonatal meningitis. Ampicillin is the treatment of choice.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Microbiology > Gram-positive rods: Listeria monocytogenes — tumbling motility, cold (psychrophilic) growth, foodborne + transplacental, intracellular, at-risk hosts (neonate/pregnant/elderly/immunosuppressed), ampicillin Rx. Cross-link the bacterial-meningitis CSF table.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Don't stop at 'it's Listeria.' The asked item is TRANSMISSION. Identify bug → then answer the sub-question (foodborne).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on tumbling motility, facultative intracellular, pleocytosis, psychrophilic (cold growth), transplacental? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Respiratory droplets (D) baits because most meningitis bugs spread that way — but those are diplococci/coccobacilli, not a tumbling gram-positive rod. Let the Gram morphology gate the route.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Correct (B). Reinforce: gram-positive ROD + tumbling/cold growth = Listeria; foodborne and transplacental are its two signature routes.

keywords.front: Gram-positive rod, tumbling motility, grows in cold

keywords.back: Neisseria meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae

firstAid.detail: Micro > Gram-positive rods: tumbling motility, cold growth, foodborne/transplacental, intracellular, meningitis in immunocompromised/neonates, ampicillin.
firstAid.location: Micro > Gram-positive rods: tumbling motility, cold growth, foodborne/transplacental, intracellular, meningitis in immunocompromised/neonates, ampicillin.

firstAid.detail: Micro/Neuro: CSF profiles — bacterial (↑PMNs, ↑protein, ↓glucose) vs viral vs fungal/TB; age-based common organisms.
firstAid.location: Micro/Neuro: CSF profiles — bacterial (↑PMNs, ↑protein, ↓glucose) vs viral vs fungal/TB; age-based common organisms.

firstAid.detail: Micro: arthropod-borne (malaria/Lyme/Rickettsia), bloodborne (HBV/HCV/HIV), droplet (Neisseria/pneumococcus), STI (Treponema/GC/Chlamydia) — match organism to route.
firstAid.location: Micro: arthropod-borne (malaria/Lyme/Rickettsia), bloodborne (HBV/HCV/HIV), droplet (Neisseria/pneumococcus), STI (Treponema/GC/Chlamydia) — match organism to route.

memoryHook: Listeria 'tumbles' out of the cold fridge into food — dangerous for transplant/pregnant/neonatal hosts; treat with ampicillin.
